description: Physics CLIL tasks for Telegram miniapp. Duolingo-style microtasks in English. 6 topics, ~120 tasks.

common_fields.feedback_wrong: hint on wrong answer (not full solution)

lessons.title: Quantities and units
lessons.intro: Learn the basic quantities used in physics and their units.

tasks.prompt: Match each quantity with its SI unit:
Match each unit with its symbol:
What is a 'quantity' in physics?
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. A quantity = something you can measure (e.g. length, mass).
tasks.feedback_wrong: A quantity is something measurable. The 'result' is called a value.

tasks.prompt: Match each term with its definition:
right_items: a standard amount used for measuring
the result of measuring a quantity

tasks.prompt_template: A ruler is an instrument ___ is used to measure length.
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Use 'that' or 'which' to define what something does.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Defining clauses use 'that' or 'which': instrument THAT/WHICH...

tasks.prompt_template: A balance is a device ___ measures mass.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Same rule: noun + that/which + verb.

tasks.prompt: Choose the correct definition of 'thermometer':
options: A thermometer is an instrument which measures temperature.
A thermometer it measures the temperature.
A thermometer is a temperature that measures.
Thermometer which a device measures temperature.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Definition pattern: [term] is a [class] which [does what].

tasks.prompt: Sort these into Quantity or Unit:

tasks.prompt_template: A stopwatch is an instrument that ___ time.
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. After 'that/which' use the verb in present simple.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Use present simple: a stopwatch MEASURES time.

tasks.prompt: Which sentence uses the prefix correctly? (1 km = 1000 m)
options: A kilometre is one thousand metres.
A kilometre is one hundred metres.
A kilometre is one millionth of a metre.
A kilometre is one tenth of a metre.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Prefix 'kilo-' always means 1000.

lessons.title: Density
lessons.intro: Compare materials by how much mass fits in a given volume.

tasks.prompt: What does density measure?
options: How much mass is in a given volume
tasks.feedback_wrong: Density tells us how much mass fits in a certain volume.

tasks.prompt: The density of water is 1 g/cm³. How is this read aloud?
tasks.feedback_wrong: The slash means 'per'. g/cm³ = 'gram per centimetre cubed'.

tasks.prompt_template: Steel is ___ than aluminium. (comparative of 'dense')
tasks.feedback_wrong: Short adjective + '-er': dense → denser.

tasks.prompt_template: Osmium is the ___ metal in the Periodic Table. (superlative of 'dense')
tasks.feedback_wrong: Superlative: short adjective + '-est'.

tasks.prompt: Densities (kg/m³): lead 11300, aluminium 2700, copper 8900, tin 7300. Steel = 7700. Sort:
Which is the densest? lead 11300, gold 19300, platinum 21400, silver 10500 (kg/m³)
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Platinum has the highest value: 21400.
tasks.feedback_wrong: 'Densest' = highest density. Look for the biggest number.

tasks.prompt: Match the adjective with its comparative form:

tasks.prompt_template: Gold (19300) is denser ___ silver (10500).
tasks.feedback_wrong: After a comparative form, always use 'than'.
tasks.language_tip: Comparative pattern: X is [adj+er] than Y

tasks.prompt: Put the steps to measure density of a stone in order:
items: Find the mass of the stone on a balance
Fill a measuring cylinder halfway with water
Record the initial volume of water
Put the stone in the water
Calculate volume = new volume − initial volume

tasks.prompt: A block has mass 200 g and volume 50 cm³. What is its density?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Density = mass ÷ volume = 200 ÷ 50.

tasks.prompt_template: Ice floats on water because ice is ___ dense than water.
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. 'Less dense' = lower density. Less dense things float.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Opposite of 'more dense' is 'less dense'.
tasks.language_tip: Comparison: 'more X' / 'less X' for longer adjectives

tasks.prompt: 100 cm³ of mercury has a mass of 1360 g. What is its density?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Density = mass ÷ volume = 1360 ÷ 100.

lessons.title: Movement vocabulary
lessons.intro: Learn the key words used to describe how things move.

tasks.prompt: Match each word with its meaning:
Match each verb with its meaning:
right_items: to work out a numerical value

tasks.prompt_template: An equation is a formula ___ shows how quantities are related.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Defining clause: noun + that/which + verb.
tasks.language_tip: Use 'that' or 'which' to define what something is

tasks.prompt: Which sentence is a correct definition?
options: Speed is a noun which tells us how fast something is moving.
Speed which is a noun tells how fast moving.
A noun speed that tells fast moving.
Speed it is fast moving thing.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Definitions follow: [term] is a [class] which/that [function].

tasks.prompt: Sort these words by word class:
A car is 'decelerating'. What does this mean?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Prefix 'de-' often means 'down/reduce'.

tasks.prompt: Match the description to the type of motion:

lessons.title: Distance-time graphs
lessons.intro: Read graphs that show how distance changes over time.

tasks.prompt: On a distance-time graph, what does the gradient (slope) tell us?
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Gradient on a distance-time graph = speed.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Gradient = change in distance ÷ change in time = speed.

tasks.image_description: Distance-time graph with a horizontal line at d=50m from t=0 to t=10s
tasks.prompt: What does this graph show?
options: The object is moving at constant speed
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Horizontal line = distance doesn't change = stationary.
tasks.feedback_wrong: If distance doesn't change with time, the object isn't moving.

tasks.image_description: Distance-time graph with a straight diagonal line from (0,0) to (10,50)
tasks.prompt: What does this graph show?
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Straight line = constant gradient = constant speed.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Straight diagonal line means the speed is not changing.

tasks.image_description: Distance-time graph with an upward-curving line (gradient increasing)
tasks.prompt: What does this graph show?
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Curve getting steeper = speed increasing = accelerating.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Steeper gradient means faster speed. Curve gets steeper → speed grows.

tasks.prompt_template: Look at this graph! The car ___ at constant speed right now. (present continuous of 'travel')
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Present continuous = action happening now.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Action happening NOW → use 'is/are + verb-ing'.

tasks.prompt_template: A horizontal line on a distance-time graph ___ that the object is not moving. (present simple of 'mean')
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. We use present simple for general facts.
tasks.feedback_wrong: General fact / rule → use present simple. 3rd person: verb + 's'.

lessons.title: Speed calculations
lessons.intro: Calculate speed, distance and time using the speed formula.

tasks.prompt: What is the formula for speed?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Speed = how much distance per unit of time = distance ÷ time.

tasks.prompt: A car travels 100 m in 5 s. What is its speed?
tasks.feedback_wrong: speed = distance ÷ time = 100 ÷ 5.

tasks.prompt: A runner covers 200 m in 25 s. Find the speed.

tasks.prompt_template: If you double the distance and keep the time the same, the speed will ___. (verb)
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Speed and distance are directly proportional.
tasks.feedback_wrong: If distance × 2, then speed × 2 (same time).
tasks.language_tip: First conditional: If + present, ... will + verb

tasks.prompt_template: If a bike travels 60 m in 10 s, its speed ___ 6 m/s. (verb 'be')
tasks.feedback_wrong: Present statement or future result both work here.

tasks.prompt: Put these in order to solve: 'A car travels 240 m in 12 s. Find the speed.'
items: Identify what is given: distance = 240 m, time = 12 s
Choose the formula: speed = distance ÷ time

tasks.prompt: A train travels at 30 m/s for 60 s. What distance does it cover?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Rearrange: distance = speed × time.

lessons.title: Acceleration
lessons.intro: Acceleration tells us how quickly speed changes.

tasks.prompt: What does acceleration measure?
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Acceleration = rate of change of speed.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Speed = how fast. Acceleration = how fast the speed CHANGES.

tasks.prompt: What is the unit of acceleration?
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. m/s² = metres per second per second.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Speed change (m/s) per second → m/s ÷ s = m/s².

tasks.prompt: Match verbs with their opposites:

tasks.prompt_template: When a car speeds up, its velocity ___. (synonym of 'increases')
tasks.feedback_wrong: Synonyms of 'increase': rise, grow, go up.

tasks.prompt: A car's speed changes from 10 m/s to 30 m/s in 5 s. What is the acceleration?
tasks.feedback_wrong: acceleration = (final − initial) ÷ time = (30 − 10) ÷ 5.

tasks.prompt: Classify each situation:

tasks.prompt_template: If the brakes are applied, the speed ___. (verb meaning to go down)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Brakes → slowing → speed decreases / falls / drops.

lessons.title: Mass and weight
lessons.intro: Mass and weight sound similar but mean different things.

tasks.prompt: What is mass?
options: The amount of matter in an object
The pull of gravity on an object
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Mass = amount of matter. Measured in kilograms.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Mass = matter. Weight = pull of gravity.

tasks.prompt: What is weight?
options: The amount of matter in an object
The force of gravity acting on an object
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Weight is a force, measured in newtons (N).
tasks.feedback_wrong: Weight = gravity pulling on the object's mass.

tasks.prompt: Sort these properties:

tasks.prompt_template: Mass is measured in kilograms, ___ weight is measured in newtons. (contrast word)
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. All three contrast connectors work here.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Use 'but', 'while', or 'whereas' to show contrast.
tasks.language_tip: Contrast: but / while / whereas (link two opposite ideas)

tasks.prompt: Which sentence correctly contrasts mass and weight?
options: Mass stays the same anywhere, whereas weight changes with gravity.
Mass stays the same anywhere and weight also stays the same.
Mass changes with gravity, while weight stays the same.
Mass it is the same as weight.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Mass = constant. Weight = depends on gravity.

tasks.prompt: On Earth, g = 10 N/kg. What is the weight of a 5 kg object?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Weight = mass × gravitational field strength = 5 × 10.

tasks.prompt_template: An astronaut has the same mass on the Moon, ___ his weight is much less. (contrast word)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Contrast: but / however / while / whereas.

lessons.title: Types of force
lessons.intro: Forces have many names — friction, weight, tension, thrust...

tasks.prompt: Match each force with its description:
right_items: the pulling force in a rope or string
the pull of gravity on an object
the pushing force from an engine
the force that slows things sliding

tasks.prompt: What is the unit of force?
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Force is measured in newtons.
tasks.feedback_wrong: kg = mass, m = length, J = energy. Force = newtons.

tasks.prompt_template: Force ___ measured in newtons. (passive: present simple of 'measure')
tasks.feedback_wrong: Passive present: subject + is/are + past participle. 'Force is measured'.

tasks.prompt_template: Newton's laws ___ used to describe forces. (passive: present simple of 'use')
tasks.feedback_wrong: Plural + passive: subject + 'are' + past participle.

tasks.prompt: Sort these forces:
Which sentence uses the passive correctly?
options: The book is pulled down by gravity.
Gravity pulls down the book is.
Gravity is the book pulled down.
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Passive: thing + is/are + past participle + by + agent.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Passive structure: [object] is/are [past participle] (by [agent]).

lessons.title: Pressure
lessons.intro: Pressure depends on force AND the area it acts on.

tasks.prompt: What is the formula for pressure?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Pressure = force per unit area = force ÷ area.

tasks.prompt: Unit of pressure?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Pressure = force (N) ÷ area (m²) = N/m².

tasks.prompt_template: If you stand on one foot, the pressure ___ increase. (modal verb)
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. First conditional: if + present, ... WILL + verb.
tasks.feedback_wrong: First conditional structure: If [present], [will + verb].
tasks.language_tip: 1st conditional: If + present simple, ... will + verb

tasks.prompt_template: If the area is smaller, the pressure ___ greater. (will + verb 'be')
tasks.feedback_wrong: First conditional: If [present], [will + verb].

tasks.prompt: A 200 N force acts on an area of 4 m². What is the pressure?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Pressure = force ÷ area = 200 ÷ 4.

tasks.prompt: Why does a sharp knife cut better than a blunt one?
options: It has a smaller area, so the pressure is higher.
It has a larger area, so the pressure is higher.
It has more force, so the pressure is lower.
It is made of a different metal.
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Same force, smaller area → higher pressure.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Pressure = force ÷ area. Smaller area → bigger pressure.

tasks.prompt_template: If a man stands on a board with many cans, the pressure on each can ___ small. (will + 'be')
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Force is spread over a larger total area.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Many cans = larger total area → smaller pressure on each.

lessons.title: Forms of energy
lessons.intro: Energy comes in many forms — kinetic, potential, thermal...
lessons.physics_target: naming and recognising forms of energy

tasks.prompt: Match the form of energy to its description:
right_items: energy stored in food and fuels
energy of an object lifted up

tasks.prompt: A moving car has what kind of energy?
tasks.feedback_wrong: Anything moving has KINETIC energy.

tasks.prompt_template: A ball on a high shelf has gravitational potential energy ___ it has been lifted up. (cause connector)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Use 'because' to give a reason / cause.

tasks.prompt_template: The wind is moving, ___ it has kinetic energy. (effect connector)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Use 'so' or 'therefore' to introduce an effect / result.

tasks.prompt: Classify each statement:
items: A stretched spring has elastic potential energy
A burning candle has chemical energy
A flying bird has kinetic energy

tasks.prompt: Choose the correct cause-and-effect sentence:
options: The water is hot, therefore it has thermal energy.
The water is hot because it has thermal energy.
Therefore the water is hot, it has thermal energy.
Because the water has thermal energy therefore it is hot.
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Cause comes before 'therefore', then the effect.
tasks.feedback_wrong: [Cause], therefore [effect]. Or: [Effect] because [cause].

lessons.title: Energy transfer and conservation
lessons.intro: Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only changed from one form to another.

tasks.prompt: What is the law of conservation of energy?
options: Energy can be created but not destroyed
Energy can be destroyed but not created
Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Total energy stays the same — it just changes form.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Conservation = total energy is constant. It changes form, not amount.

tasks.prompt: Match the device to the main energy transfer:

tasks.prompt_template: A kettle ___ electrical energy into thermal energy. (verb 'convert', present simple)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Verbs: convert, change, transform.
tasks.language_tip: convert / change / transform X INTO Y

tasks.prompt_template: In a solar panel, light energy is ___ into electrical energy. (passive past participle of 'transfer')
tasks.feedback_wrong: Passive: is + past participle. transfer → transferred.

tasks.prompt: Put the energy chain for a falling ball in order:
A 100 J of chemical energy in fuel converts to 30 J of kinetic energy. Where did the rest go?
options: It became thermal and sound energy
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. 'Lost' energy is usually wasted as heat and sound.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Energy is never destroyed — usually it becomes thermal energy (heat).

lessons.title: Heat and temperature
lessons.intro: Heat and temperature are not the same thing!

tasks.prompt: What does temperature measure?
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Temperature = AVERAGE kinetic energy of particles.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Temperature = average energy. Heat = total energy.

tasks.prompt: What is the SI unit of temperature?
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. SI unit = kelvin. 0 K = absolute zero.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Celsius is common but the SI unit is the kelvin.

tasks.prompt: Match the everyday word to the scientific word:

tasks.prompt_template: When you heat water, the average kinetic energy of the particles ___. (verb)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Heating → particles move faster → kinetic energy increases.

tasks.prompt: Classify the statements:
Which scientific sentence is correct?
options: The cup is warmer because thermal energy was transferred to it.
The cup is warmer because it has more cold.
The cup is warmer because temperature went into it.
The cup is warmer because heat made cold leave.
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Thermal energy is TRANSFERRED — there is no 'cold'.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Cold isn't a thing — it's the absence of thermal energy.

lessons.title: Kinetic theory of matter
lessons.intro: All matter is made of tiny particles in constant motion.

tasks.prompt: Match the state with particle behaviour:
right_items: particles move freely and quickly, far apart

tasks.prompt: Why do gases fill any container?
options: Because their particles are very large
Because their particles move freely in all directions
Because gases are heavier than air
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Free-moving particles spread out to fill the space.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Gas particles move freely → they spread out everywhere.

tasks.prompt_template: A solid has a fixed shape ___ its particles cannot move from their positions. (cause word)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Reason → 'because'.

tasks.prompt_template: Gas particles move freely, ___ a gas fills its container. (effect word)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Result → 'so' or 'therefore'.

tasks.prompt: Put these states in order of INCREASING particle energy:
Choose the correct explanation:
options: Ice melts because the particles gain energy and break out of fixed positions.
Ice melts because the particles get bigger.
Ice melts because new particles appear.
Ice melts because the particles disappear.
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Heating gives particles enough energy to flow past each other.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Heating → particles gain energy → they move enough to flow = liquid.

lessons.title: Wave properties
lessons.intro: Waves carry energy without carrying matter.

tasks.prompt: Match each wave property with its meaning:
right_items: the height of the wave from middle to top

tasks.prompt: Match the quantity with its unit:

tasks.prompt_template: Frequency ___ defined as the number of waves per second. (verb 'be')
tasks.feedback_wrong: Definitions: X IS defined as Y.
tasks.language_tip: X is defined as Y = formal definition

tasks.prompt: Sound 1 has higher amplitude than sound 2. Sound 1 is...
tasks.feedback_wrong: Amplitude = loudness. Frequency = pitch.

tasks.prompt: Sound A has higher frequency than sound B. Sound A is...
tasks.feedback_wrong: Frequency controls PITCH. Amplitude controls LOUDNESS.

tasks.image_description: Sine wave with large amplitude and 3 complete cycles in the time window
tasks.prompt: Compared to a quieter sound at the same pitch, this wave has...
tasks.feedback_wrong: Louder sound = bigger amplitude (taller wave).

tasks.prompt_template: The wavelength of a wave is ___ as the distance between two peaks. (passive: defined)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Definitions use the passive: 'is defined as'.

lessons.title: Sound and musical instruments
lessons.intro: Different instruments produce sound in different ways.

tasks.prompt: Match the instrument with its family:
Sort the instruments by family:

tasks.prompt_template: Sound ___ produced by vibrations. (passive 'be' + past participle)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Passive: subject + is/are + past participle.

tasks.prompt_template: Drums ___ played by hitting the surface. (passive)
tasks.feedback_wrong: Plural passive: 'are' + past participle.

tasks.prompt: Which sentence is correct passive present simple?
options: A guitar is played by plucking its strings.
A guitar plays by plucking its strings.
A guitar plucked its strings by playing.
A guitar is plucking by player strings.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Passive: [thing] is/are + past participle + by [agent].

tasks.prompt: How do wind instruments produce sound?
options: By vibrating a column of air inside
tasks.feedback_right: Correct. Air inside the tube vibrates and produces sound.
tasks.feedback_wrong: Wind instruments = air column vibrates inside the tube.
